Elkhart Lake Water Quality Data

One place to follow the health of our lake. Sensors watch the water, the weather, and the lake level. Pick a topic to dive in, or start with the overview below.

Our lake
Live right now · 2026

Happening on the lake this minute

These six readings are streaming from the lake right now, refreshing on their own all day long. Water quality comes from the buoy; air temperature and sunlight come from the weather station on shore. Scroll down for the full 2026 season and how it compares with 2024 and 2025.

Meet the sensor

What do these measurements mean?

New to these terms? Tap any signal to learn what it means, and why it matters for the lake and for you.

What the data is telling us

Reading the trends

A few things stand out across two seasons of continuous monitoring.

How the signals connect

The lake is one system

When one thing changes, others follow. These numbers show how strongly each pair of signals moved together in 2025 (−1 = opposite, 0 = unrelated, +1 = in lockstep).

Read it like a friendship chart: deep blue squares move together, deep orange squares move in opposite directions.

In plain terms

How to read the water quality data

Trust patterns, not single points

The buoy takes a reading every 30 minutes, and a passing wave, air bubble, or bit of floating debris can cause a brief spike. We smooth the readings into one typical value per day, so look for trends over days and weeks instead of trusting any single point.

What "below detection" means

When a value is too low for the sensor to measure reliably, we honestly label it "below detection." This does not mean the amount is exactly zero, just that it is too small to pin down a precise number.

Pigment color, not toxins

The algae sensors measure pigment color, which tells us how much algae is present, but they do not measure toxins. Confirming a harmful bloom always needs a separate lab test.

Colored bands are context

The colored bands on the clarity and algae charts, like "clear," "moderate," and "high," are general context to help you read the numbers. They are not legal limits or swim-closure rules.

The sky above the lake

Weather Above Elkhart Lake

A Tempest weather station at the lake reads the air every minute. Weather drives the water: sun and calm air grow algae, storms cloud the water, and wind mixes it. Here is the 2025 season, the air-side companion to the water story above.

Meet the sensor

What do these measurements mean?

Reading the weather trends

Where weather meets water

The two halves of this page tell one story. A few ways the sky and the lake connect:

How to read the weather station data

Look at the shape, not the spike

One strange reading is usually just a passing gust or a cloud drifting by. Watch how the line moves over several days, because the overall shape tells the real story.

Why you see gaps

The station lost power and sensor function from mid October 2025 through winter, so this view sticks to the reliable summer to fall window. The blank spots are simply times when no data came in.

Bars for totals, lines for trends

Rainfall and lightning show up as daily bars, since they add up as totals or counts for each day. Temperature, wind, and the rest appear as daily lines so you can follow how they rise and fall.

How weather shapes the lake

Sunny, calm, warm spells can help algae grow, while storms wash nutrients in and cloud the water. Wind stirs and mixes the lake, so these charts help explain what the water is doing.

Water level monitor

How High Is the Lake?

The level sensor is installed at one fixed point on Elkhart Lake and reads the water around the clock. It captures the fluctuation at that single spot, how much the water rises and falls through the year with rain, snowmelt, and dry spells, rather than the lake's actual depth. It is the longest-running and most up-to-date feed on the page.

More about this chart:
Detailed adds the explainer and how-to-read below.
Meet the sensor

What does this measure?

Tap to learn what lake level is and why it matters for the community.

Reading the level trends

What moves the lake level

How to read the water level data

It tracks rise and fall

This sensor shows how the water at one spot goes up and down over time, not how deep the whole lake is. Watch the way the line moves, not the exact number it lands on.

Numbers are relative, not depth

The inches are measured against a fixed mark near the sensor, so they show change rather than an official lake depth or elevation. A higher or lower number just means the water moved up or down from that mark.

The line follows the weather

Rain and melting snow usually push the level up within a day or two. Hot, dry stretches slowly draw it back down, so the line tells the story of recent weather.

A foot of swing is normal

For a lake like this, rising and falling about a foot across the seasons is healthy and expected. The goal is to notice unusual highs or lows, not to worry about the everyday ups and downs.

Citizen Lake Monitoring

Two Lakes in One: Top to Bottom

For years, volunteers in the Wisconsin Citizen Lake Monitoring Network have measured Elkhart Lake from the surface toward 100 feet down (temperature casts reached deepest; oxygen casts usually stopped shallower). In summer the lake splits into stacked layers. Pick a measurement and step through the dates to watch it happen.

More about this chart:
Detailed adds the explainer and how-to-read below.
Meet the instruments

What do these measurements mean?

Tap any signal to learn what it is and why it matters for the lake.

What the depth profiles reveal

How it all connects

How to read a depth profile

Depth runs downward

The chart is built like the real lake. The surface sits at the top and the deep water at the bottom, so reading down the page is reading down into the lake.

Each line is one day

A profile is a single sampling trip, top to bottom. Use the slider to step through the dates and watch the layers build in summer and break apart in fall.

Look for the cliff

When the line lurches sideways over just a few feet, that is the thermocline. It is the wall where the warm upper lake meets the cold deep lake.

A few times a year, for years

Volunteers sample a handful of times each summer, so the dots are sparse. The power is in the long record, stretching from 2015 to 2023.

Live from the dock

Meet the sensor

What this one number is telling you

How this reading is measured

A dock sensor, not the buoy

This live number comes from a DockSensIQ sensor mounted right at the dock, separate from the water-quality buoy out in the lake. Think of it as the temperature at the shoreline, where people actually get in the water.

Near the surface

The sensor sits near the surface in shallow, sun-touched water. On a hot, calm afternoon that shoreline water can read a little warmer than the open, deeper lake, which is exactly what a swimmer feels first.

Live, every few minutes

The reading refreshes on its own throughout the day. The time stamp above tells you how fresh the current number is, usually just a few minutes old.

Want the long view?

For temperature history and how it ties to clarity and algae, visit the Water Quality Buoy tab. For top-to-bottom profiles across many years, see Citizen Monitoring.
